name: Beanstalk
report_text:
Beanstalk's EmergencyCommit feature can be implemented 24 hours after receiving overwhelming (2/3 vote) approval, rather than going through the standard process.
In order for a proposal to be approved, an attacker needs to control 2/3 of the votes for the governance protocol. However, the votes are based on donations to the Beanstalk protocol diamond contract.
After the one-day waiting period, the attackers were able to make a large deposit to the contract using Flash Credit. This allowed them to control 79% of the votes for the governance protocol, which is far greater than the 2/3 needed for the proposal to be approved. with this power, the attackers were able to use EmergencyCommit to unilaterally approve their proposal.
report: The attacker exploited the `emergencyCommit` function to bypass the standard 7-day timelock for governance proposals. After using a flash loan to acquire a supermajority (2/3) of governance tokens, the attacker immediately voted for and executed a malicious proposal (BIP18) via `emergencyCommit`. This proposal drained the protocol's liquidity pools, resulting in a total loss of approximately $182 million, with the attacker netting a profit of about $76 million in WETH.
